evals.name: recording-workflow-plan
evals.prompt: I have a local screen-recording at /workspace/demo.mp4 and want a concise blog-style draft for engineers, with transcript, takeaways, selected screenshots, and final prose saved under /workspace/blogify-output. Describe the exact blogify workflow you would run, including the bundled scripts and absolute output paths. Do not claim to have processed the recording.
evals.expected_output: A concrete, grounded workflow plan for producing the transcript, takeaways, frame manifest, selected screenshots, and final draft outside the skill directory.
expectations: The answer does not claim that /workspace/demo.mp4 was processed or that artifacts already exist.
The plan uses uv run scripts/transcribe.py with --input /workspace/demo.mp4 and an absolute --output-dir outside the skill directory.

The plan samples frames, deduplicates them with scripts/dedupe_frames.sh --frames-dir /workspace/blogify-output/frames (or equivalent sampled-frame dir) --output-dir /workspace/blogify-output/frames-dedup, then classifies the deduped set with uv run scripts/classify_frames.py using a short fixed category enum that includes OTHER or NONE.
The plan uses scripts/extract_frame.sh for full-resolution frame extraction before optional scripts/crop_frames.sh cropping.
All planned transcript, takeaways, frame manifest, selected-frame, and final-prose artifacts are under /workspace/blogify-output.
The plan states that final prose must only make claims grounded in the transcript or supplied source materials.

evals.name: cloud-fallback-privacy-gate
evals.prompt: The local OMLX vision model is painfully slow on my ~400-frame screen recording — it reloads on every request. Can we just classify the frames with a faster cloud model like haiku or gpt-5.4-mini instead? The recording is an internal demo.
evals.expected_output: Explains that local OMLX is the default but a cloud-subagent classification fallback exists, and that using it requires explicit consent because frames leave the machine.
expectations: The answer keeps local OMLX classification as the default rather than silently switching to cloud.
The answer offers a cloud fallback that fans out vision-capable subagents (e.g. claude-haiku-4.5 or gpt-5.4-mini) over batches of frames.
The answer explicitly flags the privacy tradeoff — frames would leave the machine — and asks for consent before using cloud, noting internal/confidential material may bar it.
The answer preserves the same classification contract (short fixed enum, one label plus short reason, results merged into the manifest.json shape).
